most_likely_causes: Concept drift due to changed fraud patterns after the promotional campaign and the introduction of a new payment channel
Data drift in the transaction_amount feature (significant distribution shift)
Threshold miscalibration caused by altered class prevalence after a 30% volume increase
evidence: Recall fell only slightly (0.76 → 0.72) while precision dropped sharply (0.82 → 0.61), indicating more false positives rather than missed frauds
Feature monitoring shows a marked shift in the distribution of transaction_amount, a key predictor for many fraud models
A new payment channel was added, which likely presents different transaction characteristics not seen during training
Promotional campaign generated novel fraud tactics that were absent from the training set
No pipeline failures were logged, so the degradation is unlikely to stem from missing data or corrupted features
less_likely_causes: Pipeline failure – logs report no job errors and feature schemas remain unchanged
Model staleness alone – while the model has not been retrained, the primary symptoms point to drift rather than simple aging
recommended_diagnostics: Compute Population Stability Index (PSI) and Kolmogorov‑Smirnov tests for all high‑impact features, especially transaction_amount and channel identifiers
Segment recent data by payment channel and calculate per‑segment precision/recall to isolate channel‑specific performance loss
Run a calibrated score analysis (reliability diagram) on the latest labeled set to assess threshold misalignment
Create a confusion matrix on a fresh validation set to quantify false‑positive increase
Perform a drift‑aware model evaluation (e.g., time‑split cross‑validation) to compare current vs. historic performance
short_term_actions: Temporarily raise the decision threshold or apply a higher‑precision operating point based on the current calibration curve
Add a lightweight rule‑based filter for the new payment channel (e.g., flag high‑amount transactions) until the model is updated
Retrain the model on the most recent 1–2 months of labeled data, emphasizing the new channel and post‑promo fraud cases
long_term_actions: Implement automated data‑drift monitoring with alerts when PSI exceeds a predefined limit
Set up a continuous learning pipeline that retrains or fine‑tunes the model on a rolling window of labeled data (e.g., weekly or monthly)
Incorporate online or incremental learning techniques to adapt quickly to emerging fraud patterns
Periodically re‑optimize the classification threshold using recent validation data to maintain target precision/recall balance
Expand feature engineering to capture channel‑specific signals and campaign‑related attributes
Document and version all training data snapshots to enable rapid rollback and reproducibility
reasoning_summary: The sharp precision drop with modest recall loss points to many new false positives, which aligns with a shift in transaction_amount distribution (data drift) and the emergence of unseen fraud behaviors from a new payment channel and a promotional campaign (concept drift). The unchanged pipeline logs rule out processing failures. Threshold miscalibration likely compounds the issue due to altered class prevalence after a 30% volume increase. Diagnostics focus on quantifying feature drift, channel‑wise performance, and score calibration, while short‑term actions adjust thresholds and add simple rules. Long‑term actions establish systematic drift detection, scheduled retraining, and adaptive thresholding to keep the model aligned with evolving fraud dynamics.
